title: Instrumentality and its allusions to communism v capitalism
display_title: Instrumentality and its allusions to communism v capitalism
wikitext:
[]: Instrumentality and its allusions to communism v capitalism Post by jimothy_183 » Sun Oct 16, 2011 12:13 am I was watching a youtube video of a quartet version of Canon in D from Death and Rebirth and a comment from the video got me wondering. It goes: Snickers5261 wrote: I have a question? How does this song relate to instrumentality? Does this song, as used in the movies, represent instrumentality, or the beauty of individuality. Is it supposed to mean that individuals, by harnessing their differences (different instruments) can work together to create beauty.﻿ Or does it mean that beauty is achieved through harminous cooperation and singularity. (instrumentality)

[]: Post by Cosman246 » Sun Oct 16, 2011 12:28 am Oh, please, don't politicize it that way. I see it more as Hegel vs. Kierkegaard. "Gee, capitalism is FTW! Individualism equals capitalism FTW!"--I've seen far too many people who end up in that trap Capitalism does not equal individualism. Communism does not equal collectivism. There are far too many subphilosophies in both to count. Keynes and Rand were both capitalists (though Keynes was an economist and Rand was a proselytizer); Trotsky and Stalin were both communists

[]: Post by Allemann » Sun Oct 16, 2011 8:28 am Cosman246 wrote: Capitalism does not equal individualism.

[]: Post by Cosman246 » Sun Oct 16, 2011 11:39 am Again, there's far too many subphilosophies in both. "Capitalism" isn't really a philosophical stance as much as it is an economic system built around markets--and yes, regulated markets are built around markets, so they do count as capitalism. With Communism, you have all sorts of schools, most (but not all!) of which are Marxist (which itself began as a theory of economic history lightly inspired by the Hegelian dialectic)--and very few of which have ever gained power. I believe that Sartre is a good example of an individualist Marxist. And also, capitalism and communism don't exist in a vacuum. You also have all the other historical economies, such as feudalism.

[]: Post by Allemann » Sun Oct 16, 2011 12:31 pm Cosman246 wrote: Again, there's far too many subphilosophies in both.

[]: Post by Cosman246 » Sun Oct 16, 2011 1:00 pm What I am saying here is merely that they're not actually individualist or collectivist in themselves--it all really depends on one's interpretation of society. Marx himself denies that the individual really has any importance in a capitalist society, only the "bourgeois individual"--and that the rest are collectivized by way of corporate institutions, alienated from their labor. (Note: As for my political leanings, I'm a general lefty) I could also use the same "corporatism isn't really capitalism" argument to say that there has never been a truly Communist society. Most of the so-called communist societies functioned with markets and classes, though in a different societal arrangement than western democracies.

[]: Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Oct 16, 2011 1:34 pm The big dichotomy between capitalism and communism is the ownership of the means of production. In Complementation, the answer to the question of "who should own the means of production?" is a resounding "Mu!" -- as is true in the more limited forms of the unimind, where the constituent sophonts are still embodied e.g the scenario of Sturgeon's To Marry Medusa ; or the various intermittently awakened worlds of Stapledon's Star Maker cosmos, let alone in the post-corporeal setting of the Human Complementation Project.

[]: Post by Cosman246 » Sun Oct 16, 2011 1:58 pm I couldn't have said it better. I think the reason that Complementarity may *seem* similar to Communism is because of the debt of both to Hegel. If you want, we can start a thread about that.

[]: Post by SaVaGe » Sun Oct 16, 2011 2:05 pm Capitalism vs Communism is just one situation Eva can be applied to. But there are a million others. That's what happens when you leave so much of a series up to self interpretation.

[]: Post by NemZ » Sun Oct 16, 2011 2:38 pm Allemann wrote: How is this making a problem to give an example of a collectivist market economy and an indvidualistic communist society?

[]: Post by BobBQ » Sun Oct 16, 2011 3:21 pm "You're all idiots. Study harder."

[]: Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Oct 16, 2011 3:24 pm NemZ wrote: For the first, I don't see any reason why a company can't compete within the general capatalist market but remain internally a community of equal share holders regardless of job title.

[]: Post by Sachi » Sun Oct 16, 2011 5:25 pm Evangelion is much more akin to the "Collectivist vs. Individualist" argument than to the "Communist vs. Capitalist" argument. Sure, many themes of the former argument exist in the latter, but there's still enough distinction to keep them separated. For example, one important theme in Capitalism is competition; progress is made when two entities compete with each other to produce a better good, which naturally leads to advances to technology, etc. But if we apply this theme to the Individualist side of Evangelion, it doesn't fit quite as well. What form of competition is reflected in the every day lives of our main characters? Perhaps it exists as the competition to define oneself as an individual, but that seems like a major stretch to me.

[]: Post by NemZ » Sun Oct 16, 2011 5:55 pm the show answers that for you, Sachi. RITSUKO: Homeostasis and transistasis. Misato: What are those? RITSUKO: One is a force for the status quo, and the other's a force for change. A living thing is something that has both of these conflicting qualities.

[]: What? B/ Post by Alaska Slim » Sun Oct 16, 2011 8:30 pm Sachi wrote: Evangelion is much more akin to the "Collectivist vs. Individualist" argument than to the "Communist vs. Capitalist" argument.

[]: Re: What? B/ Post by Fireand'chutes77 » Sun Oct 16, 2011 8:58 pm Alaska Slim wrote: Case in point, in Gunbuster , the representation of the Western world was the Soviet Union.

[]: Post by chaosakita » Sun Oct 16, 2011 11:37 pm I am usually open-minded about readings of different texts, but I would say here that this reading is pretty stupid. Not everything can be read from a Marxist theory perspective, nor should everything be. In any case, Tokyo-3 remains a capitalistic society, and the characters speak highly of the innovation (presumably driven by capitalism) that has restored it back into a thriving city. I suppose that Anno would be against the consumerism that drives capitalism and forces people to be "inauthentic" (whatever that means), but nothing in Eva would be considered a specific incitement about capitalism. Nor does it really say anything about communism. Anyways, as a writer, I would just say that some works are clearly not meant to be political, and I think Eva would definitely be one of them.

[]: Post by Trajan » Mon Oct 17, 2011 1:34 pm I'm just going to pile on and say someone badly misinterpreted the main themes of the series.

[]: Post by CJD » Mon Oct 17, 2011 3:00 pm Trajan wrote: I'm just going to pile on and say someone badly misinterpreted the main themes of the series.

[]: Post by Ornette » Mon Oct 17, 2011 3:15 pm CJD wrote: I can see how he made the mistake, though, and I don't think it's uncommon. I wish I could remember where I read it, but wasn't there some writer or commentator who said something similar, that Eva had to do with capitalism vs communism? Maybe someone else knows what I'm talking about, I can't remember who.
